title: Reported Speech Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Reported speech (also called indirect speech) describes what someone said without quoting their exact words - this usually requires shifting the tense back one step, changing pronouns, and adjusting time/place words. Here are 50 common mistakes with reported speech, explained simply, with the correct transformation.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he is tired.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he was tired.
commonMistakes.why: When the reporting verb ('said') is in the past tense, the tense inside the reported clause usually shifts back one step too - present simple ('is') becomes past simple ('was'). This shift is called 'backshifting', and it applies to almost all reported statements about the past.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that she is going to the market.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she was going to the market.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, present continuous ('is going') shifts back to past continuous ('was going') when reporting what someone said in the past. Keep applying this same backshift rule consistently across all present tenses.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me that he will help me.
commonMistakes.right: He told me that he would help me.
commonMistakes.why: The future tense marker 'will' shifts back to 'would' in reported speech, following the same backshifting pattern as other tenses. Remember this specific shift: will becomes would when reporting past speech.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that she can swim well.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she could swim well.
commonMistakes.why: The modal verb 'can' shifts back to 'could' in reported speech, just as other tenses shift back one step. Memorize this modal shift alongside will/would: can becomes could.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he has finished the work.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he had finished the work.
commonMistakes.why: Present perfect ('has finished') shifts back to past perfect ('had finished') in reported speech. This follows the same backshifting logic - present perfect moves back to past perfect, just as present simple moves to past simple.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that she went there yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she had gone there the day before.
commonMistakes.why: This has two fixes: past simple ('went') often shifts back to past perfect ('had gone') in reported speech, and the specific time word 'yesterday' must change to 'the day before' since we're no longer speaking on that same day. Whenever reporting speech from a different day, adjust both the tense and any time expressions.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked me did I like the movie.
commonMistakes.right: He asked me if I liked the movie.
commonMistakes.why: Reported yes/no questions need 'if' or 'whether' added, and the word order returns to normal statement order (no more question inversion). Never use direct question word order (did I) inside reported speech - always convert to statement order with if/whether.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked me where do I live.
commonMistakes.right: She asked me where I lived.
commonMistakes.why: Just like yes/no questions, reported WH-questions (where, what, why) also drop the question word order and inversion, returning to normal statement order. Remove 'do/does/did' entirely and shift the tense onto the main verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said, I will call you tomorrow, he said this yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: He said he would call me the next day.
commonMistakes.why: This example shows multiple needed shifts together: 'will' becomes 'would', 'you' becomes 'me' (since the reporter is the one being called), and 'tomorrow' becomes 'the next day' since we're not speaking on the same day as the original statement. Reported speech often requires several simultaneous shifts: tense, pronoun, and time word.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me that she is coming today.
commonMistakes.right: She told me that she was coming that day.
commonMistakes.why: This combines two shifts: 'is coming' becomes 'was coming' (backshift), and 'today' becomes 'that day' since the reporting happens at a different time than the original statement. Whenever reporting speech later, adjust time words that refer to 'now' into words that refer to 'then'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he will come here.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he would come there.
commonMistakes.why: Besides 'will' shifting to 'would', the place word 'here' must shift to 'there' since the reporter is typically not in the same location as the original speaker was referring to. Remember this place shift alongside the tense shift: here becomes there in reported speech.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said she must leave immediately.
commonMistakes.right: She said she had to leave immediately. / She said she must leave immediately (both acceptable).
commonMistakes.why: 'Must' (meaning obligation) can shift to 'had to' in reported speech, though 'must' can also stay unchanged in many cases, especially for strong obligation or rules. This modal is a partial exception - it has some flexibility in reported speech, unlike 'will' and 'can', which always shift.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked, "What time is it?" and I said I don't know.
commonMistakes.right: He asked what time it was, and I said I didn't know.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence needs the reported question to be properly converted with statement word order, and the response also needs the standard backshift from 'don't' to 'didn't'. Apply the backshift rule to every reported clause in a sentence, not just the first one.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that this is her book.
commonMistakes.right: She said that was her book. / She said that this was her book.
commonMistakes.why: Beyond the tense shift (is to was), the demonstrative pronoun 'this' often shifts to 'that' in reported speech when referring to something not physically present at the time of reporting. Consider shifting 'this/these' to 'that/those' when reporting speech at a later time or in a different place.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said he would come, but he said it yesterday and now it's tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: He said he would come.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence is unnecessarily complicated - reported speech doesn't need you to explain the exact timing of the original statement separately; the backshifted tense ('would') already implies this happened in the past relative to now. Trust that the backshifted verb tense already carries the necessary time relationship, without over-explaining.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked me can I help her.
commonMistakes.right: She asked me if I could help her.
commonMistakes.why: This combines two fixes: add 'if' since this is a yes/no question, and shift 'can' back to 'could' following the standard modal backshift rule. Both fixes (adding if/whether AND backshifting the modal) are usually needed together in reported yes/no questions.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me that he doesn't like coffee.
commonMistakes.right: He told me that he didn't like coffee.
commonMistakes.why: Present simple negative ('doesn't like') shifts back to past simple negative ('didn't like') in reported speech, following the same backshift rule as positive sentences. Negative sentences follow exactly the same backshifting pattern as positive ones - don't forget to shift these too.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that she isn't coming to the party.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she wasn't coming to the party.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, present continuous negative ('isn't coming') shifts to past continuous negative ('wasn't coming'). Keep applying the backshift rule consistently to every verb form you encounter, whether positive or negative.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said he has never been to Paris.
commonMistakes.right: He said he had never been to Paris.
commonMistakes.why: Present perfect ('has been') shifts to past perfect ('had been') in reported speech, even with 'never' inserted in the middle. The presence of 'never' or other adverbs doesn't change the backshift rule - it still applies to the main verb tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me she is going to study medicine.
commonMistakes.right: She told me she was going to study medicine.
commonMistakes.why: The 'going to' future form shifts back to its past tense version ('was going to') in reported speech, just like other present tenses. Remember that 'going to' future follows the same backshift pattern as present continuous, since it uses the same verb structure.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked where I am going.
commonMistakes.right: He asked where I was going.
commonMistakes.why: Even in this embedded question (already in normal statement order), the tense still needs to backshift from present ('am going') to past ('was going'). Don't forget the tense shift just because the question word order was already correct - both fixes are usually needed together.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said, "I have already eaten," so she said she has already eaten.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she had already eaten.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence incorrectly keeps the present perfect ('has eaten') instead of shifting to past perfect ('had eaten'), which is required since the original statement itself was already in present perfect. When the original quote is in present perfect, the reported version shifts to past perfect.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me I should to call him back.
commonMistakes.right: He told me I should call him back.
commonMistakes.why: This is a separate mistake unrelated to backshifting - 'should' is a modal verb, and modal verbs are never followed by 'to' before the base verb. Remove 'to' after any modal verb (should, can, will, must), whether in reported speech or a direct statement.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked if I was here yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: She asked if I had been there the day before.
commonMistakes.why: This needs several shifts together: 'was' becomes 'had been' (past shifts to past perfect when reporting an even earlier past event), 'here' becomes 'there', and 'yesterday' becomes 'the day before'. Complex reported speech often requires multiple simultaneous shifts - practice identifying all of them in one sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he would came tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he would come the next day.
commonMistakes.why: This has two issues: after 'would', the verb must stay in base form ('come'), not past tense ('came'), and 'tomorrow' should shift to 'the next day'. Always check the verb form after modals like would/could/should - they need the base verb, never a past tense form.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me that she needs my help.
commonMistakes.right: She told me that she needed my help.
commonMistakes.why: Present simple ('needs') shifts back to past simple ('needed') in reported speech, following the standard backshift rule. This is a simple, regular example of the core backshift rule applying to an ordinary present simple verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked me what I am doing here.
commonMistakes.right: He asked me what I was doing there.
commonMistakes.why: This needs two shifts: the tense backshifts from present continuous ('am doing') to past continuous ('was doing'), and the place word shifts from 'here' to 'there'. Combine the tense shift and place shift together whenever both apply in the same sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that tomorrow she will visit her grandmother.
commonMistakes.right: She said that the next day she would visit her grandmother.
commonMistakes.why: This needs both the time word shift ('tomorrow' to 'the next day') and the modal shift ('will' to 'would'), applied together in the same sentence. Whenever a sentence has both a time word and a future tense marker, remember to shift both.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me he can't come today.
commonMistakes.right: He told me he couldn't come that day.
commonMistakes.why: This needs both the modal shift ('can't' to 'couldn't') and the time word shift ('today' to 'that day'), combined in one sentence. Practice looking for multiple elements needing change within a single reported sentence - modals, time words, and place words often appear together.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked whether I have finished my work.
commonMistakes.right: She asked whether I had finished my work.
commonMistakes.why: Even though 'whether' correctly introduces this reported yes/no question, the tense still needs to shift from present perfect ('have finished') to past perfect ('had finished'). Using 'whether' or 'if' correctly doesn't exempt you from also applying the tense backshift.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said he is having lunch right now.
commonMistakes.right: He said he was having lunch at that time.
commonMistakes.why: This needs both the tense shift (present continuous to past continuous) and the time phrase shift ('right now' to 'at that time'), since 'right now' only makes sense from the original speaker's perspective at the moment of speaking. Time phrases describing the present moment ('now', 'right now', 'currently') typically shift to phrases describing a past moment when reporting later.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me that her sister lives here.
commonMistakes.right: She told me that her sister lived there.
commonMistakes.why: Present simple ('lives') shifts to past simple ('lived'), and 'here' shifts to 'there' since the reporter is describing a location relative to the original speaker's perspective. Whenever a place word appears in reported speech, consider whether it needs to shift along with the tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked if I will be free next week.
commonMistakes.right: He asked if I would be free the following week.
commonMistakes.why: This needs both the modal shift ('will' to 'would') and the time phrase shift ('next week' to 'the following week'). Always check for both a tense/modal shift and a time-word shift together whenever future time references appear in reported speech.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said, "I bought this yesterday," so she said she bought this yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she had bought that the day before.
commonMistakes.why: This example needs multiple shifts together: past simple ('bought') often shifts to past perfect ('had bought'), 'this' shifts to 'that', and 'yesterday' shifts to 'the day before'. When reporting speech that already contains time and demonstrative words, expect several simultaneous shifts.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me he doesn't want to go there anymore.
commonMistakes.right: He told me he didn't want to go there anymore.
commonMistakes.why: Present simple negative ('doesn't want') shifts back to past simple negative ('didn't want'), following the standard rule even with the word 'anymore' included. Additional words like 'anymore' or 'still' don't change the basic backshift rule for the main verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked me if I am ready to leave.
commonMistakes.right: She asked me if I was ready to leave.
commonMistakes.why: Even with 'if' correctly used for this yes/no question, the verb 'am' still needs to shift back to 'was' in reported speech. Using the correct question-reporting word ('if') doesn't replace the need for tense backshifting.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he was working on it and he'll finish soon.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he was working on it and that he would finish soon.
commonMistakes.why: The second half of this sentence still needs the future shift from 'will' to 'would', even though the first half was already correctly shifted. When a sentence has multiple clauses being reported, check that EACH one has the necessary tense shifts, not just the first.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me that she is thinking about moving abroad.
commonMistakes.right: She told me that she was thinking about moving abroad.
commonMistakes.why: Present continuous ('is thinking') shifts to past continuous ('was thinking'), following the standard backshift pattern once more. This is a straightforward, regular application of the core backshift rule for present continuous tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked me what time does the store close.
commonMistakes.right: He asked me what time the store closed.
commonMistakes.why: This needs two fixes: remove 'does' since this is now an embedded/reported question with normal word order, and shift the tense onto the main verb ('closed') directly. Combine the word-order fix (statement order, no do/does/did) with the tense backshift for a fully correct reported question.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said she has been feeling unwell lately.
commonMistakes.right: She said she had been feeling unwell lately.
commonMistakes.why: Present perfect continuous ('has been feeling') shifts to past perfect continuous ('had been feeling') in reported speech. This shows the backshift rule extending to the more complex continuous perfect tenses as well, not just simple tenses.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me he will always remember this day.
commonMistakes.right: He told me he would always remember that day.
commonMistakes.why: This needs both the modal shift ('will' to 'would') and the demonstrative shift ('this day' to 'that day'), applied together. Whenever 'this' appears alongside a future tense marker, expect to shift both elements when reporting.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked if I can meet her tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: She asked if I could meet her the next day.
commonMistakes.why: This combines the modal shift ('can' to 'could') with the time word shift ('tomorrow' to 'the next day'), both required together. This exact combination - modal plus time word - appears very frequently in reported speech exercises.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he is planning to visit next month.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he was planning to visit the following month.
commonMistakes.why: This needs both the tense shift (present continuous to past continuous) and the time phrase shift ('next month' to 'the following month'). Keep practicing this combination until identifying both necessary shifts becomes automatic.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me she doesn't know the answer.
commonMistakes.right: She told me she didn't know the answer.
commonMistakes.why: This is a simple, direct application of the core backshift rule: present simple negative shifts to past simple negative. Practicing simple, clear examples like this one helps reinforce the fundamental rule before tackling more complex combined shifts.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked whether I was coming to the meeting today.
commonMistakes.right: He asked whether I was coming to the meeting that day.
commonMistakes.why: Even though the tense ('was coming') is already correctly in past continuous, the time word 'today' still needs to shift to 'that day' since we're reporting this at a different time. Don't forget to check time words even when the tense itself is already correctly shifted.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said that she can't believe the news.
commonMistakes.right: She said that she couldn't believe the news.
commonMistakes.why: This is a direct, simple application of the modal backshift rule: can't shifts to couldn't. This example reinforces the modal-shift rule in its most straightforward form.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me he is not sure about the plan.
commonMistakes.right: He told me he was not sure about the plan.
commonMistakes.why: Present simple with 'be' ('is not sure') shifts to past simple ('was not sure'), following the same rule as all other present tense forms. This shows the backshift rule applying even to simple descriptive statements using 'be', not just action verbs.

commonMistakes.wrong: She asked if I have any questions.
commonMistakes.right: She asked if I had any questions.
commonMistakes.why: Present simple ('have') shifts to past simple ('had') in this reported yes/no question, combining the 'if' structure with the standard tense backshift. This is one of the most common reported speech patterns in everyday conversation, especially in meetings and interviews.

commonMistakes.wrong: He said that he was here now.
commonMistakes.right: He said that he was there then.
commonMistakes.why: Both 'here' and 'now' need to shift to 'there' and 'then' respectively, since the reporter isn't speaking from the same place or moment as the original speaker. Whenever both a place word and a time word appear together in the original quote, shift both consistently.

commonMistakes.wrong: She told me that this was the best day of her life.
commonMistakes.right: She told me that that was the best day of her life.
commonMistakes.why: The demonstrative 'this' shifts to 'that' in reported speech when referring to something no longer immediate or present. Practice shifting demonstratives (this/these to that/those) alongside tense shifts whenever they appear in the original statement.

quiz.question: Choose the correct reported speech:
options: He said that he is tired.
He said that he was tired.
He said he is being tired.
quiz.answer: He said that he was tired.
quiz.explanation: When the reporting verb is past tense, the reported clause usually shifts back one tense too.

quiz.sentence: He told me that he will help me.
quiz.correction: He told me that he would help me.
quiz.explanation: 'Will' shifts back to 'would' in reported speech.

quiz.question: Choose the correct reported question:
options: He asked me did I like the movie.
He asked me if I liked the movie.
He asked me if did I like the movie.
He asked me I liked the movie.
quiz.answer: He asked me if I liked the movie.
quiz.explanation: Reported yes/no questions need 'if' or 'whether', with normal statement word order.

quiz.question: She said she ___ (can/swim) well. (backshift 'can')
quiz.explanation: 'Can' shifts back to 'could' in reported speech.

quiz.question: How does 'tomorrow' typically change in reported speech?
quiz.explanation: Time words shift to match the new time perspective of the reporter.
